title: Rectified Linear Unit
description: A simple activation function that keeps positive values and turns negative values into zero.
openingSummary: The rectified linear unit (ReLU) is a simple activation function that leaves positive values alone and turns negative values into zero, making it a useful baseline for understanding how later activation variants trade simplicity for smoother or leakier behavior.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: The rectified linear unit (ReLU) is an activation function used in many neural networks. It applies one simple rule to each value: keep positive numbers and replace negative numbers with zero. In transformer feed-forward layers, that rule decides which hidden features stay active before the layer projects back to the model width.

whatItOptimizes.title: What It Optimizes
whatItOptimizes.body: ReLU optimizes for a very cheap and very sharp nonlinearity. It is easy to compute, easy to implement, and naturally creates sparse activity because only positive values pass through fully.

practicalBenefit.title: Practical Benefit
practicalBenefit.body: ReLU gives readers a clean reference point. Once you understand its hard zero cutoff, it becomes much easier to see what later variants change when they add a small negative path, a smoother curve, or a learned gate.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: ReLU works one value at a time. If the value is positive, it stays as it is. If the value is negative, it becomes zero. In a transformer feed-forward layer, the model first expands into a wider hidden state, applies that rule to each hidden value, and then projects the filtered result back down.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: The formula below shows the ReLU rule for one value at a time. The key detail is the hard cutoff at zero.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: ReLU is the sharpest cutoff in this activation family. Leaky rectified linear unit keeps a faint negative path, while sigmoid linear unit smooths the whole transition instead of using a hard corner.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: ReLU mattered most in earlier deep networks and remains a common baseline in papers, even though many newer language models now prefer smoother or gated feed-forward activations.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: Because every negative value becomes zero, ReLU can throw away weak negative evidence completely. That simplicity helps efficiency, but it also makes the activation less smooth and less expressive than many newer alternatives.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: ReLU remains the cleanest reference point for activation discussions. When a paper says an activation is smoother, leakier, or more selective, ReLU is usually the baseline sitting underneath that comparison.

computeFlow.alt: Activation comparison chart showing how rectified linear unit, leaky rectified linear unit, and sigmoid linear unit treat positive and negative values
computeFlow.caption: This chart compares the hard zero cutoff in ReLU, the small negative slope in LeakyReLU, and the smooth negative-side shrinkage in SiLU.

comparisonTable.caption: How rectified linear unit compares with nearby activation variants

relu.negativeBranch: Clamp every negative hidden value to 0
relu.mainTradeoff: Very simple and sparse, but it throws away all negative signal

leakyRelu.negativeBranch: Keep a small slope alpha x for x < 0
leakyRelu.transitionShape: Hard corner with a weak negative path
leakyRelu.mainTradeoff: Preserves gradient on the negative side, but stays less smooth than SiLU

silu.negativeBranch: Shrink negative values smoothly with x sigma(x)
silu.mainTradeoff: Smoother hidden filtering, but less minimal than ReLU

x.definition: One input value before the activation rule is applied.

negativeBehavior.label: Negative values stop here: x < 0 becomes 0
